texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The removal of CO2 from natural and bio gases is an important step to agree with the pipeline standards. Membrane-based gas separation technology  is  attracting  more  and  more  attention  for  CO2 capture because it offers the superiorities in efficiency, operation cost and convenience  [1].  Zeolite  membranes  with  uniform  and  angstrom-scale channels are promising for gas separation [2 -4].

texts.orig: Small-pore eight-membered ring (8 MR) zeolite membranes, such as CHA [5 -16], DDR [17 -19], ERI [20] and AEI [21] with pore sizes of 3.6 -4.1 Å, are suitable for the separation of CO2 (3.3 Å) from CH4 (3.8 Å). The zeolite CHA membranes include aluminophosphate analog of SAPO-34 [5,7,22]  and  aluminosilicate  analog  of  low-silica  chabazite [8], high-silica CHA (namely SSZ-13) [9 -12] and all-silica CHA [13 -16]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: High-quality tubular SSZ-13 zeolite membranes were fabricated by a facile in-situ approach for the first time. The in-situ approach requires only a single hydrothermal step for membrane growth, which is much simpler than the normal multi-step secondary-growth approach with seed preparation and seeding steps. The proper aging time and relatively high synthesis temperature provide sufficient nuclei for SSZ-13 growth, which further facilitates the in-situ formation of continuous membranes on the substrates. Three SSZ-13 zeolite membranes fabricated by the simple in-situ approach displayed ultrahigh average selectivity of (233 ± 46) and high average carbon dioxide permeance of (1220 ± 100) × 10 9 mol (m 2 s Pa) 1  [~3660 ± 300 Gas Permeation Unit (GPU), 1 GPU = 3.348 × 10 10 mol/(m 2 s Pa)] at 298 K in the CO2/CH4  (50/50 in mol) mixture. The overall separation performance of the current membranes exceeded that of most reported zeolite membranes in the CO2/CH4 mixture. It  indicated  that the current in-situ synthesis approach had good reproducibility. The typical membrane also showed excellent separation performance in the CO2/N2 mixture toward the carbon capture from flue gas. The long-term stability of this membrane was investigated in the humidified CO2/CH4  mixture with a relative humidity close to 100%. This simple in-situ approach is suitable to prepare other zeolite membranes for gas separation after improving the gel nucleation.

texts.orig: membranes.  CHA-type  zeolite  membranes  have  shown  outstanding separation performance in the CO2/CH4  [23 -25] and CO2/N2  [26,27] mixtures due to 3.8 Å × 3.8 Å channels. SAPO-34 membranes showed high selectivity (60) and high carbon dioxide permeance [1000 × 10 9 mol (m 2 s Pa) 1 ] in the CO2/CH4  mixture at 4.6 MPa [28]. However, hydrophilic SAPO-34 and low-silica chabazite membranes were impermeable to any gas containing water vapor [8,29]. It is because hydrophilic  pores  of  zeolites  were  easy  to  be  plugged  by  preferentially adsorbed water molecules. Hydrophobic silica-rich zeolite membranes, including zeolite SSZ-13 and Si-CHA, were considered to be resistant to water vapor [30].

[9] first prepared SSZ-13 membranes that displayed CO2/CH4  selectivities of 10 -20 in 2002 by secondary growth. Yang et al. reported the synthesis of hollow fiber SSZ-13 membranes that exhibited  CO2/CH4  selectivities  of  over  100  via  a  secondary-growth approach  [33,34].  Tang  et  al.  [32]  developed  gel-less  secondary growth for the efficient fabrication of SSZ-13 membranes with CO2/CH4 selectivities of 35 -116 on seeded supports. Yu et al. [35] and Lee et al. [36] scaled up SSZ-13 membranes on seeded 50-cm-long ceramic tubes. We previously fabricated SSZ-13 membranes by a seeded approach on monolithic and tubular supports for light gas separations [11,37 -40]. Maghsoudi et al. prepared high silica CHA zeolite membrane by triple hydrothermal processes to repair the boundary defects [41]. The first layer was formed by in-situ method, but it was not selective to gases. The other  two  hydrothermal  processes  were  secondary  (seeded)  growth since the existed zeolite layer acts as seeds. The membrane showed CO2 and H2S permeances of 33.9 and 17.0 × 10 9 mol (m 2 s  Pa) 1 and CO2/CH4 and H2S/CH4 selectivities of 21.6 and 3.24, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The common secondary-growth synthesis approach includes at least four  steps:  seed  preparation,  seed  suspension  preparation,  seed  layer preparation and final hydrothermal synthesis step as shown in Scheme 1. The muti-step procedures for a secondary-growth approach increased the complexity and cost and reduced the synthesis efficiency. The singlestep in-situ synthesis approach is much simpler and more attractive than the secondary-growth method since the first three steps are omitted as shown in Scheme 1. The facile in-situ synthesis could be credible to prepare zeolite membranes once the optimized synthesis parameters are found. The insitu synthesis approach was successfully conducted for the fabrication of MFI [42] and LTA [43] zeolite membranes whose crystallization rates were  relatively  fast. However,  high-silica  small-pores  uniform  zeolite membranes  for  gas  separation  has  not  yet  been  reported  by  an  in-situ approach .  The  challenges  are  that  the  crystallization  rates  of  such  a class of zeolites, including SSZ-13, are very slow. Controlling the uniform nucleation of these zeolites on the support is the key point to obtain a continuous membrane by an in-situ approach.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Herein, we have presented a novel single-step in-situ approach to synthesize continuous and thin SSZ-13 membranes for the first time. The nucleation and subsequent crystallization rate of this zeolite in gel were improved after rational synthesis modification, including the increase of aging time and synthesis temperature. Moreover, the sufficient nuclei from the gel facilitated the formation of continuous membranes in a short time. The in-situ crystallization processes of this high-silica zeolite and membrane were investigated in detail. The typical SSZ-13 membrane exhibited ultrahigh selectivities of 300 and 34 and carbon dioxide permeances of 1290 × 10 9 and 1540 × 10 9 mol (m 2 s Pa) 1 for binary CO2/CH4  (N2)  (50/50  in  mol)  mixtures  at  298  K,  respectively.  The overall separation performance of this membrane was superior to that of most  zeolite  membranes  fabricated  using  a  complicated  secondarygrowth approach. This simple synthesis method is suitable to produce other high-performance zeolite membranes with high synthesis reproducibility.
texts.text: Herein, we have presented a novel single-step in-situ approach to synthesize continuous and thin SSZ-13 membranes for the first time. The nucleation and subsequent crystallization rate of this zeolite in gel were improved after rational synthesis modification, including the increase of aging time and synthesis temperature. Moreover, the sufficient nuclei from the gel facilitated the formation of continuous membranes in a short time. The in-situ crystallization processes of this high-silica zeolite and membrane were investigated in detail. The typical SSZ-13 membrane exhibited ultrahigh selectivities of 300 and 34 and carbon dioxide permeances of 1290 × 10 9 and 1540 × 10 9 mol (m 2 s Pa) 1 for binary CO2/CH4  (N2)  (50/50  in  mol)  mixtures  at  298  K,  respectively.  The overall separation performance of this membrane was superior to that of most  zeolite  membranes  fabricated  using  a  complicated  secondarygrowth approach. This simple synthesis method is suitable to produce other high-performance zeolite membranes with high synthesis reproducibility.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: SSZ-13 membranes were fabricated by an in-situ approach on the outside surface of the commercial alumina tubes (length: 6.0 cm, OD: 12 mm, ID: 9.0 mm and porosity: ~30%) from the Membrane Park Co. The asymmetric support had 100-nm and 30μ m-thick top layer and the 1.0μ m bottom  (body)  layer.  The  effective  area  for  gas  permeation  was approximately 16 cm 2 .
texts.text: SSZ-13 membranes were fabricated by an in-situ approach on the outside surface of the commercial alumina tubes (length: 6.0 cm, OD: 12 mm, ID: 9.0 mm and porosity: ~30%) from the Membrane Park Co. The asymmetric support had 100-nm and 30μ m-thick top layer and the 1.0μ m bottom  (body)  layer.  The  effective  area  for  gas  permeation  was approximately 16 cm 2 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  a  typical  synthesis,  23.4  mmol  (0.85  g)  of  NaOH  (96%,  Sinopharm), 51.5 ml (51.5 g) of deionized water (DI) and 35 mmol (17.3 g) of  home-made  25  wt% N,N,N -trimethyl-1-adamantyl  ammonium  hydroxide  (abbreviated  as  TMAdaOH,  described  in  Ref.  [11])  aqueous solution were mixed to form a uniform solution; and then 2.3 mmol (0.08 g) of alumina hydroxide (99%, Wako) and 117.2 mmol (15.4 g) of colloidal silica with code of TM-40 from Aldrich were added into the mixture. The composition of initial synthesis mixture is 1.0 SiO2: 0.01 Al2O3: 0.2 TMAdaOH: 0.2 NaOH: 40 H2O in mole. The gel was aged for an optimized aging time of 1.0 d, which was several times longer than that (2 -6 h) of the normal aging in our previous studies [11,37,38]. Both ends of the supports were sealed by plastic inserts. The bare supports, without any surface modification or seeding, and the gel were added in a PTFE-lined autoclave. The in-situ synthesis was conducted at temperature range of 413 -473 K for 4.0 -48 h. The optimized synthesis temperature was 453 K, which was 10-20 K higher than that by secondary growth in our previous studies [11,37 -39]. The organic template was removed by a low-temperature ozone calcination process at 473 K for 48 h. The flow rate of ozone/oxygen gas was 240 ml (STP) min 1 . For convenience, the membrane code Mn-x represented the membrane synthesized at n K for x h ( n = 413, 433, 453 and 473 K, x = 4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 40 and 48 h, respectively).

texts.orig: Single-component permeation was tested in the  retentate-blocked mode and mixed-gas permeation was done in the pressure-drop mode in dry and humidified CO2/CH4 (N2) (50/50 in mol) mixtures without sweep  gas.  The  retentate-blocked  mode  was  also  called  as  deed-end mode.  The  pure  gas  was  remained  in  the  feed  side  by  blocking  the retentate  side  and  the  pressure  was  regulated  using  a  back-pressure regulator. The membrane-separation device was similar to that in our previous studies [44]. Mass flow controllers were utilized to tune the composition of the gas and the feed flow rate [normally 4.0 standard liter per minute (SLPM)]. And the humidity of the mixture was determined by the bubbling of feed gas with the relative humidity close to 100%. The flux ( J ) is measured using a bubble meter. texts.orig: The morphologies of the Px serial powder samples prepared at 453 K for 8 -48 h are shown in Fig. 1a -f. The coexistence of a large number of amorphous phases and a small number of cubic SSZ-13 crystals was observed in P-8 sample (as seen in Fig. 1a). The cubic and highly crystalline samples were obtained when synthesis time was larger than 16 h. The  crystal  size  enlarged  from  0.5  to  4.0 μ m  when  synthesis  time increased  from  12  to  48  h  (Fig.  1a -f).  The  XRD  pattern  of  Fig.  1g revealed that peaks were assigned to the aluminum source of gibbsite [Al(OH)3] rather than zeolite CHA structure, indicating that the main part of P-8 sample was amorphous phase. The typical zeolite CHA peaks increased but those of gibbsite decreased when reaction time increased from 8 to 24 h (Fig. 1g). All the powders prepared for 24 -48 h revealed highly crystalline CHA phase without any impurity phase. The in-situ crystallization curve of SSZ-13 zeolite was illustrated in Fig. 2. The induction period for SSZ-13 growth was about 8.0 h. Many SSZ-13 crystals were formed, and the crystallinity of the powder was about 80% after 16-h synthesis. It meant that SSZ-13 crystallized quickly once the nuclei were  formed  [45].  A  fully  crystalline  SSZ-13  was  formed  when  the crystallization time was longer than 16 h.

texts.orig: membranes prepared for 16 -48 h had the typical XRD peaks of CHA framework and the alumina support, suggesting that the membrane is pure  CHA  phase  without  other  impurity  zeolite  phases  as  shown  in Fig. 3. The electronic microscope of the membranes in Fig. 4 also agreed with  the  above  XRD  results.  The  bare  support  owns  an  asymmetric microstructure with a 30μ m-thick top layer (Fig. 4a and b). A small number of crystals with particle size of around 0.5 μ m were covered on the surface of alumina tube (Fig. 4c) after 8-h synthesis. It is consistent with the above results in the in-situ crystallization  of  SSZ-13  zeolite. Subsequently, the intergrowth behavior of the membrane was improved as synthesis time increased from 16 to 40 h (as observed from Fig. 3c-f). It is worth noting that aging time was improved from 2 to 6 h [46,47] to 1.0 d. The increasing aging time favors faster nucleation and crystallization [45]. Many nuclei could be transported from the gel to the surface of substrate when sufficient ones could be formed in the gel in a short time. As desired, continuous and uniform SSZ-13 zeolite membrane with thickness  of  ~3.0 μ m was formed on the alumina support by in-situ synthesis at 453 K for 40 h (Fig. 4k). The membrane thickness of the sample prepared for 48 h was much similar to that for 40 h, suggesting that  the  nutrients  in  gel  might  be  depleted  after  40-h  synthesis. Comparing in-situ crystallization  processes  of  SSZ-13  powder  and membranes, their crystallization rates were very similar. It suggested furthermore  that  the  nuclei  for  the in-situ membrane growth mainly came from the gel.

texts.orig: Fig.  5  exhibits  the  electronic  microscopes  of  SSZ-13  membranes prepared under different synthesis conditions. Temperature is one of the key  parameters  for  the  growth  of  desired  zeolite  crystals  [48]  and membranes [49]. The alumina tube was covered with a large number of spherical-like polycrystals when the membrane was in-situ synthesized at 413 K. At the same time, there were obvious pinhole defects in the membrane.  Membrane  thickness and intergrowth behavior were improved when synthesis temperature increased from 413 to 453 K as shown in Fig. 5. It indicated that a higher growth rate of zeolite layer was achieved at higher temperature. The uniformity of the membrane declined when synthesis temperature increased to 473 K as shown in Fig. 5. The thickness of membrane M-473-40 was as large as 5.8 μ m. Separation data in Table 1 confirmed the decline of membrane uniformity when synthesis temperature enhanced from 453 to 473 K. The optimized  synthesis  temperature  for  the in-situ synthesis  was  453  K, which was 10 -20 K higher than that for our previous secondary growth [11,37,38]. The membranes M-453-40-4 and M-453-40-5 were prepared for aging time of 2.0 h and 3.0 d, respectively; and the membranes were leakage before calcination. Their surface morphologies in Fig. 5f and g displayed that the intergrowth of the membranes was poor since some pinpores were observed in the membranes. It suggests that a suitable aging  time  is important  for  the in-situ formation  of  continuous membranes.

texts.orig: Fig. 6 illustrates the single-component permeances of various light gases  through  membrane  M-453-40-1  at  298  K.  Nitrogen  is  almost impermeable [ < 1 × 10 10 mol (m 2 s Pa) 1 ] in membrane M-453-40-1 before the removal of the organic template. The permeances of the gases through  calcined  membrane  M-453-40-1  decreased  with  molecular diameter except carbon dioxide. This order was same with the results for zeolite  CHA  membranes  in  the  literatures  [37,50,51].  Pure  CO2  permeance was 1320 × 10 9 mol (m 2 s Pa) 1 and ideal CO2/CH4 (N2) selectivities were 170 and 20, respectively. Ideal selectivity for H2/SF6 was up to 494, suggesting the formation of a defect-less zeolite membrane after template removal.

texts.orig: Two SSZ-13 membranes were prepared by secondary growth. The membranes  had  the  same  crystal  morphology  and  a  relatively  thick zeolite layer (~3.5 μ m as shown in Fig. 5h and i) compared with in-situ synthesized  membrane  M-453-40-2.  The  membranes  also  displayed good separation performance with an average CO2 permeance of 1550 × 10 9 mol (m 2 s Pa) 1 and an average CO2/CH4 selectivity of 130 at the standard test conditions. The permeance was about 27% higher than the current membrane but CO2/CH4 selectivity was about 45% lower than these of the current one. Uniform seed layer was hardly produced since the  submicron  seeds  could  not  be  evenly  embedded  into  the  course surface  of  the  porous  substrate.  Moreover,  the  seeds  on  the  support might be dropped in the gel due to the weakly physical bonding between the seeds and support surface or be dissolved by the attack of the base solution.  A  few  more  defects  were  formed  in  the  secondary-growth membrane  than  in  the in-situ membrane  due  to  the  uncertain  and complicated effects of seeds in the former case. As a result, the quality of the membrane fabricated by secondary growth was relatively poor.

texts.orig: Fig. 7 shows the relationships between the separation performance of membrane M-453-40-1 and pressure drop and temperature in 50/50 CO2/CH4  mixture. The membrane showed an ultrahigh selectivity of 300 at pressure drop of 0.2 MPa. This value is higher than most reported zeolite membranes [10,31,32,38,54] even though they were prepared with  the  assistance  of  seeds.  The  permeance  of  CO2  decreased  from 1290 × 10 9 to  670 × 10 9 mol  (m 2 s  Pa) 1 when  pressure  drop enhanced from 0.2 to 1.2 MPa. The decreasing trend might be due to the fact that the CO2 loading on the surface crystals was close to saturation in the investigated pressure range [55]. At the same time, concentration polarization, which always decreased the permeance of fast gas (herein CO2) but increased that of slow gas (herein CH4) became more critical at higher pressures. It could be taken into account for the declined CO2 permeance  and  selectivity  of  CO2/CH4 [56].  Methane  permeance improved slightly with increasing pressure drop because CH4  mainly went through non-zeolitic pores and the mass transport rates increased with pressure [55]. As a consequence, the separation selectivity declined with the increasing pressure drop. The selectivity and CO2 permeance of membrane M-453-40-1 declined as temperature increased in the investigated temperature range in the CO2/CH4 mixture (Fig. 7b). texts.orig: Fig. 8 displays the relationships of separation performance of membrane M-453-40-1 with the parameters of pressure drop and temperature in 50/50 CO2/N2  mixture. The selectivity and CO2  permeance of membrane M-453-40-1 were 34 and 1540 × 10 9 mol (m 2 s Pa) 1 at pressure  drop  of  0.2  MPa,  respectively,  in  the  CO2/N2  mixture.  The selectivity and CO2 permeance reduced from 34 to 17 and from 1540 to 720 mol (m 2 s Pa) 1 with enhancing pressure drop from 0.2 to 1.2 MPa, respectively. The trends of separation parameters in the CO2/N2 mixture were the same as those in the CO2/CH4  one. The selectivity and CO2 permeance of membrane M-453-40-1 in the CO2/N2  mixture were 10 and 590 × 10 9 mol (m 2 s Pa) 1 at 423 K, respectively. The separation performance were superior to those of most other membranes [8,10,45, 58,59].

texts.orig: Fig. 9 shows separation performance of SSZ-13 membrane M-453-403 in the CO2/CH4  mixture with saturated water vapor at 338 K. The membrane maintained high selectivity of 103 and good CO2 permeance of 795 × 10 9 mol (m 2 s Pa) 1 . The declined separation performance was because that the pores were partially plugged by water vapor. The membrane displayed stable separation performance during the continuous 80-h operation. Please note that the feed flow rate for the stability test was 0.8 SLPM, which was much lower than that (4.0 SLPM) of the standard test in order to save gas. Moreover, separation performance of the membrane was resumed to its original level once the humidity was removed from the system using dry sweep gas at 433 K overnight. It confirmed  that  the  current  membrane  showed  excellent  separation performance and stability even in the presence of saturated water vapor.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: High-quality SSZ-13 membranes were first prepared by the simple and novel in-situ approach. The formation of sufficient nuclei in gel after rational  synthesis  modification  enables  the  production  of  uniform membrane on bare supports without seeding. Synthesis of the membrane had a good reproducibility by the facile approach. The typical SSZ-13 membrane displayed ultrahigh selectivities of 300 and 34 and carbon dioxide permeances of 1290 × 10 9 and 1540 × 10 9 mol (m 2 s Pa) 1 (3870 and 4620 GPU) and for the binary CO2/CH4  (N2) (50/50) mixtures  at  298  K,  respectively.  This  high-silica  membrane  displayed excellent stability over humidity for the continuous 80-h test. The in-situ approach was much simpler than the secondary-growth approach once the  fast  and  uniform  nucleation  and  crystallization  are  obtained  by rational synthesis modification replacing the effect of seeding. It could be a versatile and powerful synthesis approach to construct other highsilica zeolite membranes for gas separation after exactly tuning their crystallization.
